APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (Japanese predicate-argument structure / zero anaphora) using neural sequence/Grid-RNN models and demonstrates state-of-the-art gains on the standard NAIST Text Corpus with comparisons to strong baselines (Ouchi et al. 2015) and ablations (depth, residuals). The methods (stacked Bi-GRU, Grid‑RNN, residual connections), evaluation metrics (F-measure), dataset splits, and significance testing are all standard for top NLP venues. The work is clearly positioned within the relevant literature (joint vs pointwise PAS, prior neural SRL/PAS work) and targets the right audience interested in neural modeling of linguistic structure and multi-predicate interactions.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem (Japanese PAS and zero-argument resolution in pro-drop languages) and shows that neural sequence models—especially a Grid-RNN multi-sequence model—can outperform the state-of-the-art joint parser-based system without using syntactic information (paper reports ~2–3 point F improvements for zero arguments and a very large gain for DAT). By demonstrating strong, syntax-free gains on the standard NAIST Text Corpus and by explicitly modeling multi-predicate interactions, the work is likely to change how researchers approach PAS (reducing reliance on parser outputs) and to be built upon for other pro‑drop languages and SRL variants, though it is not broadly transformative across all of NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents solid empirical evaluation on the NAIST-1.5 corpus with standard splits, compares to strong baselines (pointwise and Ouchi et al.), uses significance testing, and includes useful ablations (single vs multi-sequence, depth and residual connections) showing gains (Sec. 6, Tables 1–2). However it omits important reproducibility details (model sizes, embedding/hidden dims, batch size, epochs, random seeds), lacks error analysis or discussion of failure cases/limitations beyond excluding inter-sentential arguments, and does not explore some sensitivity/hyperparameter analyses (e.g., choice of GRU, learning-rate effects). Related work coverage is reasonable and candid about dataset mismatches, but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than comprehensive, so I rate it a 3.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (stacked Bi-RNN single-sequence and Grid-RNN multi-sequence models improve PAS accuracy without syntactic features) are supported by experiments on the standard NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 with comparisons to reimplemented baselines (Imamura+09, Ouchi+15) and significance testing via bootstrap resampling (Sec. 6.1–6.2). The mathematical parts shown (stacked Bi-RNN update eqs and softmax output) are correct and the training setup (GRU cells, residual connections, Adam, cross-entropy loss with L2) is standard and appropriate. Minor issues are that the Grid-RNN update details are not fully specified, the rationale for replacing CRF with softmax is asserted but not deeply justified, and some implementation/hyperparameter details are omitted, but these do not undermine the reported experimental conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: Overall structure follows a logical flow (task → models → experiments) and the main idea (Grid-RNN for multi-predicate interactions) is described, but presentation quality is uneven. Notation and equations are generally clear (e.g., Eq.3, softmax output), yet many formatting artifacts and repetitions (duplicated paragraphs, inline footnote artefacts like "2The F-measure...", "TeX output 2017..."), and missing referenced figures/tables (Figure 1/3/6, Tables 1–3 are cited but not shown) hinder readability. Minor clarity issues include scattered repetitions and occasional awkward phrasing, which require cleanup for smooth reading.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Imamura et al. 2009 as pointwise and Ouchi et al. 2015 as the state-of-the-art joint model) using the same NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 and standard splits (Sec. 6.1), and reports significance testing (Sec. 6.2). It fairly acknowledges when other neural works (Shibata et al., Iida et al.) cannot be directly compared due to different/unpublished datasets and explains methodological differences to Zhou & Xu (2015). The paper also provides per-case-role and ablation analyses (network depth, residual connections) to analyze why it outperforms baselines, and cites/discusses the relevant competing approaches (Sec. 5).

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing neural components (stacked Bi-RNNs/GRUs, residual connections) and adapts Grid-RNNs to jointly model multi-predicate interactions for Japanese PAS, which is a novel application but not a fundamentally new method (see Sec. 4: "we extend the single-sequence model to the multi-sequence model using Grid-RNNs"). The problem (Japanese PAS) is well-studied and prior work (Ouchi et al. 2015, Zhou & Xu 2015) is explicitly distinguished (no syntactic features, dependency vs. constituent SRL, joint multi-predicate modeling). Overall this is a non-trivial and useful adaptation/combination of known techniques rather than a highly original theoretical contribution.
